content:
# Genesis Chapter 35 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20r–20v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 32–33

---

*God Commands Jacob: Return to Bethel (vv. 1–5)*

**35:1** And God said to Jacob, "Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there. Make an altar there to God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother Esau."

**35:2** So Jacob said to his household and to all who were with him, "Put away the foreign gods that are among you, and purify yourselves, and change your garments. **35:3** Then let us arise and go up to Bethel, so that I may make an altar there to God who answered me in the day of my distress and has been with me wherever I have gone."

**35:4** So they gave to Jacob all the foreign gods that were in their hands, and the rings that were in their ears, and Jacob buried them under the terebinth that was near Shechem.

**35:5** And they journeyed, and a terror of God was upon the cities that were around them, so that they did not pursue the sons of Jacob.

---

*Arrival at Bethel — El-Bethel — Deborah's Death (vv. 6–8)*

**35:6** And Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan — that is, Bethel — he and all the people who were with him. **35:7** And he built an altar there and called the place El-Bethel, because it was there that God had revealed himself to him when he was fleeing from his brother.

**35:8** And Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died, and she was buried below Bethel under the oak. And he called its name Allon-bacuth [Oak of Weeping].

---

*God Appears — El Shaddai — The Israel Naming Confirmed (vv. 9–13)*

**35:9** And God appeared to Jacob again when he came from Paddan-aram, and blessed him. **35:10** And God said to him, "Your name is Jacob; your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name." And he called his name Israel.

**35:11** And God said to him, "I am El Shaddai. Be fruitful and multiply. A nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come forth from your body. **35:12** The land that I gave to Abraham and to Isaac I will give to you, and to your offspring after you I will give the land."

**35:13** Then God went up from him in the place where he had spoken with him.

---

*Jacob's Massebah at Bethel (vv. 14–15)*

**35:14** And Jacob set up a massebah [standing-pillar] in the place where he had spoken with him, a pillar of stone. And he poured out a drink offering on it and poured oil on it. **35:15** And Jacob called the name of the place where God had spoken with him Bethel.

---

*Journey from Bethel — Rachel's Death (vv. 16–20)*

**35:16** And they journeyed from Bethel. And when there was still some distance to go to Ephrath, Rachel went into labor, and her labor was severe. **35:17** And when her labor was at its hardest, the midwife said to her, "Do not fear, for you have another son."

**35:18** And as her soul was departing — for she was dying — she called his name Ben-oni [Son of My Sorrow]. But his father called him Benjamin [Son of the Right Hand].

**35:19** And Rachel died, and she was buried on the way to Ephrath, which is Bethlehem. **35:20** And Jacob set up a massebah [standing-pillar] over her grave. That is the massebah of Rachel's grave to this day.

---

*Israel Journeys — Reuben's Sin (vv. 21–22a)*

**35:21** And Israel journeyed on and pitched his tent beyond the tower of Eder. **35:22** And while Israel was dwelling in that land, Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine. And Israel heard.

---

*The Twelve Sons of Jacob (vv. 22b–26)*

And the sons of Jacob were twelve.

**35:23** The sons of Leah: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, and Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun. **35:24** The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin. **35:25** The sons of Bilhah, Rachel's servant: Dan and Naphtali. **35:26** The sons of Zilpah, Leah's servant: Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan-aram.

---

*Jacob Comes to Isaac — Isaac's Death (vv. 27–29)*

**35:27** And Jacob came to his father Isaac at Mamre, at Kiriath-arba, which is Hebron, where Abraham and Isaac had sojourned. **35:28** Now the days of Isaac were a hundred and eighty years. **35:29** And Isaac breathed his last and died and was gathered to his people, old and full of days. And his sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "A terror of God" — chittat Elohim (v. 5): EMET's construct form preserved (3-0)**

All three translators agree on the meaning. EMET preserves the Hebrew construct "a terror of God" (chittat Elohim), which is the most literal rendering and preserves the ambiguity between a superlative genitive ("a godly/enormous terror") and a genitive of source ("a terror sent by God"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both interpret as "a terror from God," specifying directionality that the Hebrew does not explicitly mark. SOPHIA adopts EMET's "a terror of God" as closer to the Hebrew construct. The LXX's *ekstasis theou* ("astonishment of God") is noted as an interpretive broadening.

**2. El Shaddai untranslated (v. 11): EMET/LOGOS upheld, PNEUMA overruled (2-1)**

Per established project convention (confirmed at Genesis 17:1, 28:3), El Shaddai is preserved untranslated. EMET and LOGOS both follow this convention. PNEUMA renders "El Shaddai — God Almighty" in the text, adding an interpretive gloss that the convention explicitly avoids. SOPHIA rules 2-1 with EMET/LOGOS: El Shaddai stands without translation in the text. The LXX's omission of *Shaddai* entirely (rendering simply "I am your God") confirms that the name's meaning was already uncertain or untranslatable in antiquity.

**3. Massebah preserved with bracketed gloss (vv. 14, 20): Convention applied (3-0)**

Per established convention, the term massebah is preserved with a bracketed gloss "[standing-pillar]" rather than translated outright. All three translators acknowledge the term's significance. EMET brackets it as "standing-pillar [מַצֵּבָה]"; LOGOS renders "pillar" throughout; PNEUMA uses "stone pillar" and "pillar." SOPHIA applies the convention: "massebah [standing-pillar]" in both verse 14 (the Bethel consecration pillar) and verse 20 (Rachel's grave marker), maintaining consistency with prior chapters.

**4. "And Israel heard" — no addition (v. 22): EMET upheld (2-1)**

The MT reads simply vayyishma' Yisra'el — "and Israel heard" — with no object specified. EMET renders this exactly. LOGOS adds "of it" and PNEUMA adds "about it," both supplying an implied object that the Hebrew deliberately omits. The omission is part of the text's literary effect — the abrupt, objectless verb followed by the setumah paragraph break creates the silence that is the point of the verse. SOPHIA rules 2-1 with EMET (and effectively with LOGOS, who agrees the silence is the text's meaning even while supplying "of it"): no object is added. "And Israel heard." Period.

**5. "Full of days" — yamim (v. 29): EMET/LOGOS upheld, PNEUMA overruled (2-1)**

The Hebrew reads שְׂבַע יָמִים (*seva' yamim*) — literally "satisfied/full of days." EMET and LOGOS both render "full of days," preserving the Hebrew idiom exactly. PNEUMA renders "full of years," which is a reasonable interpretive equivalent but loses the specific Hebrew word *yamim* (days). The phrase is a fixed patriarchal formula (cf. Genesis 25:8 for Abraham), and consistency across the patriarchal death notices requires the same rendering. SOPHIA rules 2-1: "full of days."

**6. Ben-oni glossed as "Son of My Sorrow" (v. 18): PNEUMA's primary etymology adopted (2-1)**

The Hebrew *on/oni* is genuinely polysemous (sorrow OR strength), as EMET extensively documents. However, the narrative context — Rachel is dying, the narrator explains "for she was dying" — overwhelmingly supports the grief reading. LOGOS preserves the name without gloss; EMET brackets both etymologies; PNEUMA renders "Son of My Sorrow." SOPHIA adopts a bracketed gloss with the primary (contextually dominant) reading: "[Son of My Sorrow]" — while EMET's notes on the polysemy remain available for scholarly reference. The LXX confirms the sorrow reading (*Huios odunēs mou*, "Son of my pain").

**7. Kivrat-ha'arets rendered as "some distance" (v. 16): LOGOS/PNEUMA approach adopted (2-1)**

The term is genuinely obscure — a hapax legomenon with no established etymology or cognate. EMET transliterates as "a kivrat-ha'arets" with an extensive bracketed note. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "some distance," which communicates the narrative sense (they were not yet at Ephrath) without claiming a precision the Hebrew does not support. SOPHIA adopts "some distance" as the readable consensus, noting in the disagreements file that EMET's transliteration preserves more of the Hebrew's strangeness.

**8. "God went up from him" (v. 13): EMET/LOGOS followed over PNEUMA (2-1)**

EMET renders "God went up from upon him" (*vayyaal Elohim me'alav*); LOGOS renders "God went up from him"; PNEUMA renders "God withdrew from the place." The Hebrew verb עלה (*alah*, "to go up, to ascend") specifically denotes upward motion — God ascends, rises, goes up. "Withdrew" flattens the spatial theology: God ascends from the place of encounter, returning to the divine realm above. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "God went up from him."

**9. Earrings — no interpolated gloss in translation text (v. 4): EMET/LOGOS followed (2-1)**

PNEUMA adds "for these were pagan amulets" after mentioning the earrings — an interpolation not present in the Hebrew. EMET and LOGOS both render the verse without such an addition, reserving the amulet interpretation for their notes. The cultic function of the earrings is a reasonable inference but belongs in commentary, not in the translation text. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the rings that were in their ears" without interpolation.

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 32–33)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 35:5 | חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים ("terror of God") | ἔκστασις θεοῦ ("astonishment of God") | Follow MT: "terror of God" |
| 35:7 | נִגְלוּ (plural verb with Elohim) | ὤφθη (singular verb) | Follow MT; note anomalous plural in EMET notes |
| 35:7 | אֵל בֵּית-אֵל ("El-Bethel") | Βαιθηλ ("Bethel") | Follow MT: "El-Bethel" — compound divine name preserved |
| 35:8 | אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת ("Allon-bacuth" / Oak of Weeping) | βαλανος πενθους ("oak of mourning") | Follow MT transliteration with bracketed gloss |
| 35:11 | אֵל שַׁדַּי ("El Shaddai") | ἐγώ εἰμι θεός σου ("I am your God") | Follow MT: El Shaddai untranslated |
| 35:16 | כִּבְרַת הָאָרֶץ (obscure distance) | ἱππόδρομος γῆς ("horse-course of land") | Render as "some distance" |
| 35:18 | בֶּן-אוֹנִי ("Ben-oni") | Υἱὸς ὀδύνης μου ("Son of my pain") | Follow MT name with gloss: "[Son of My Sorrow]" |

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
